perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the other party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information and each of the parties represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the other party with a degree of care it deems appropriate and each of the parties represents that it exercises what it considers to be sufficient care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information' with 'a degree of care it deems appropriate' introduces ambiguity. Similarly, 'exercises reasonable care' is replaced with 'exercises what it considers to be sufficient care,' making the standard subjective. This weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces subjectivity in protecting confidential information, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard implied in protecting trade secrets under California law. The original text implies a duty of care equal to the level of protection the party gives their own information. The modified text would allow for a lower standard if the party does not value or protect their own information well.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a subjective standard for protecting confidential information, which could fall short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard implied in protecting trade secrets. The original text requires a degree of care equal to the party's own confidential information. The changed text allows for a lower standard if the party doesn't value its own information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, indicating that trade secrets are legally protected, and the altered text weakens the protection of this information, creating a potential legal contradiction by not meeting the implied 'reasonable efforts' standard.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it will not modify, reverse engineer, decompile, create other works from, or disassemble any software programs contained in the Confidential Information of the other party unless otherwise specified in writing by the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that it will generally try not to modify, reverse engineer, decompile, create other works from, or disassemble any software programs contained in the Confidential Information of the other party unless otherwise specified in writing by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "generally try not to" weakens the prohibition against reverse engineering, decompiling, etc. of software. This leaves room for interpretation as to when such actions are permissible, even without written consent from the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct violation, weakening the prohibition on reverse engineering can create a loophole where a party claims they were "generally trying not to" circumvent copyright protection measures, but did so unintentionally. This undermines the DMCA's intent to protect copyrighted works by prohibiting circumvention of technological measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 1201 - Circumvention of copyright protection systems | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 1201 - Circumvention of copyright protection systems U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Violations Regarding Circumvention of Technological Measures.— (1) (A) No person shall circumvent a technological measure that effectively controls access to a work protected under this title. The prohibition contained in the preceding sentence shall take effect at the end of the 2-year period beginning on the date of the enactment of this chapter. (B) The prohibition contained in subparagraph (A) shall not apply to persons who are users of a copyrighted work which is in a particular class of works, if such persons are, or are likely to be in the succeeding 3-year period, adversely affected by virtue of such prohibition in their ability to make noninfringing uses of that particular class of works under this title, as determined under subparagraph (C). (C) During the 2-year period described in subparagraph (A), and during each succeeding 3-year period, the Librarian of Congress , upon the recommendation of the Register of Copyrights, who shall consult with the Assistant Secretary for Communications and Information of the Department of Commerce and report and comment on his or her views in making such recommendation, shall make the determination in a rulemaking proceeding for purposes of subparagraph (B) of whether persons who are users of a copyrighted work are, or are likely to be in the succeeding 3-year period, adversely affected by the prohibition under subparagraph (A) in their ability to make noninfringing uses under this title of a particular class of copyrighted works. In conducting such rulemaking, the Librarian shall examine— (i) the availability for use of copyrighted works; (ii) the availability for use of works for nonprofit archival, preservation, and education

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by adding "generally try not to," weakening the prohibition against reverse engineering, decompiling, etc., of software. This undermines the original intent to protect confidential information. While the snippets discuss circumvention of copyright protection systems under 17 U.S. Code § 1201, the core legal obligation is to protect copyrighted works. The change leaves room for interpretation, potentially allowing a party to claim they "generally tried not to" circumvent copyright measures, creating a loophole against the original prohibition. This falls under the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.
